segments.title: Why the Leanders sit awkwardly between cruiser roles
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke frames the Leanders as ships caught between the scout-cruiser and first-class protected-cruiser roles.
segments.body: The comparison is a question of role fit, not a rejection of the class as attractive or capable ships. Dr. Clarke argues that light cruisers had absorbed several earlier cruiser functions, leaving the Leanders in a middle position that did not fully match either end of the older spectrum.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the class as a second-class protected-cruiser concept that sits between scout and first-class roles.

segments.title: A vanished cruiser role still shapes the design
segments.summary: The Leander concept retains the logic of a second-class protected cruiser even though that category had disappeared by the 1930s.
segments.body: In its earlier setting, a second-class protected cruiser could be exceptionally well suited to its defined role. Dr. Clarke's point is that the formal category had gone by the 1930s, while the designers and admirals shaping a new cruiser programme had learned their trade when its assumptions still mattered.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts the old second-class protected-cruiser role with its disappearance in the 1930s and the influence of earlier professional experience.

segments.title: The Leander as a baseline for a cruiser programme
segments.summary: A middle-of-the-road Leander design could give a renewed light-cruiser programme room to develop in heavier or lighter directions.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke presents the class's middle position as a sensible place to restart a light-cruiser programme. Starting from that baseline, a navy could theoretically develop a larger first-class protected-cruiser direction, represented here by the Town class, or reduce the design toward a scout cruiser.

evidence.note: The transcript calls the Leander a sensible starting point and contrasts a larger Town-class direction with a reduced scout-cruiser direction.
